subject: skl_azure-ai-voicelive-dotnet-src-https-github-com-mic-dface443-kills-azure-ai-voicelive-dotnet-skill-md_dface443
evidence: VoiceLive .NET analysis: 'Teaches .NET developers real-time voice-to-voice AI using Azure OpenAI's VoiceLive service layered on Cognitive Services Speech SDK. Streaming audio pipelines, voice model config, conversation state, error handling.' VoiceLive Java analysis: 'Teaches wiring up real-time voice conversations using Azure's AI VoiceLive Java SDK — connecting streaming audio to Azure OpenAI models, configuring voice profiles, handling bi-directional sessions.' → alternatives, weight 0.90. Both skills solve the identical core task — real-time voice-to-voice AI via Azure VoiceLive SDK — but target mutually exclusive language ecosystems (.NET/C# vs Java/JVM). A pack should choose one based on the project's language stack.

subject: skl_azure-ai-projects-java-src-https-github-com-micros-451e994c-a-skills-azure-ai-projects-java-skill-md_451e994c
evidence: Projects analysis: 'creating projects, wiring data connections, registering models, deploying artifacts. Pure infrastructure scaffolding.' Agents analysis: 'create agent with model/instructions/tools, async task management, server-side conversation state. Pick only if team map already pinned to Azure Foundry.' → chains_with, weight 0.73. Projects provisions AI Foundry infrastructure and registers model deployments; Agents consumes those provisioned models to create and run persistent AI agents on Azure Foundry. The chain is sequential: Projects provisions → Agents runs. Both share Java and Azure Foundry, making the handoff natural. The connection requires the minor inference that the models Projects registers are the same models Agents instantiates agents against, but no explicit handoff is described in either analysis.
